Glossary About
East Asian Traditions

Kuiji

632 – 682

Kuiji, also known as Ci’en Dashi, is one of the most important but still under-assimilated figures for a global history of philosophy of mind.

Kuiji, also known as Ci’en Dashi, is one of the most important but still under-assimilated figures for a global history of philosophy of mind. A rigorous reconstruction of Kuiji’s philosophy of mind should not treat him as merely a Chinese transmitter of Indian Yogācāra, nor as a simple “idealist” in the Western sense. Kuiji is better understood as a philosopher of cognitive construction, self-misrecognition, latent mental causality, semantic mediation, intersubjective influence, and soteriological transformation. His mature position, developed through the Tang Faxiang or Weishi tradition associated with Xuanzang, can be summarized as follows: ordinary experience is produced by multiple, momentary streams of consciousness; those streams carry latent “seeds” that condition perception, memory, affect, embodiment, and karmic continuity; the subject-object world is a dependent cognitive construction falsely grasped as independent; the sense of self arises through a defiled reflexive appropriation of the store consciousness; other minds are not denied, but are understood as causally effective mental continua; liberation is not the discovery of a metaphysical soul or a mind-independent object-world, but the transformation of the basis of cognition itself. Kuiji’s work stands at the intersection of metaphysics, psychology, epistemology, semantics, ethics, and contemplative practice.

This makes Kuiji historically significant in two ways. First, within East Asian Buddhism, he helped crystallize the Faxiang interpretation of Yogācāra around the Cheng weishi lun — “Treatise on the Establishment of Consciousness-Only” — and its commentarial system. Second, in a broader history of philosophy of mind, he offers a non-Cartesian, non-physicalist, anti-substantialist theory of mind centuries before the canonical Western debates over dualism, idealism, empiricism, transcendental subjectivity, phenomenology, representationalism, functionalism, and the unconscious. The comparison with the West is illuminating, but it must be handled carefully: Kuiji is not Descartes, not Berkeley, not Kant, not Hume, not Husserl, and not Freud. He shares problems with each, but his conceptual architecture is different.

1. Historical location: Kuiji and the Tang consolidation of Consciousness-Only

Kuiji lived from 632 to 682 CE. He was a Tang dynasty monk, a leading disciple of Xuanzang, and the first patriarch of the Faxiang school in the standard East Asian classification. He was born into an elite family, entered monastic life under Xuanzang’s influence, studied Sanskrit and Indian Buddhist scholasticism, and became a central figure in Xuanzang’s translation bureau. Several sources emphasize his role not merely as a passive assistant but as a major exegete and likely editor of the Cheng weishi lun, the key East Asian Yogācāra treatise that synthesizes Vasubandhu’s Thirty Verses with ten Indian commentaries, especially the Dharmapāla line favored by Xuanzang and Kuiji.

The broader setting matters. Before Xuanzang and Kuiji, Yogācāra had already entered China through earlier traditions associated with texts such as the Daśabhūmika and Mahāyānasaṃgraha, and through Paramārtha’s translations and interpretations. Xuanzang traveled to India partly to resolve doctrinal discrepancies in these materials, returned to Chang’an in 645 with a large body of Sanskrit texts, and then launched a major translation project. Through Xuanzang and Kuiji, what became known as Faxiang or Weishi — “Dharma-characteristics” or “Consciousness-only” — took shape as a highly technical Chinese Buddhist school.

The label “Faxiang” is itself philosophically revealing. The school’s own preferred designation was often Weishi, “Consciousness-only,” while “Faxiang,” “Dharma-characteristics,” could be used by opponents to mark it as a tradition focused on analytic classification of phenomena rather than on more synthetic, Buddha-nature, or emptiness-oriented approaches. This polemical naming already signals one of Kuiji’s historical roles: he stands for an analytic, scholastic, discriminating, and system-building approach to Buddhist mind theory, in contrast with traditions that preferred a more universalized Buddha-nature discourse.

Kuiji’s major works include his commentary on the Cheng weishi lun, usually referred to as the Cheng weishi lun shuji; the Dasheng fayuan yilin zhang, a large doctrinal encyclopedia; commentaries on Vasubandhu’s Twenty Verses and Thirty Verses; works on Buddhist logic; and writings connected with Maitreya devotion and Prajñāpāramitā. His work is therefore not merely metaphysical. It is exegetical, epistemological, semantic, meditative, doctrinal, and pedagogical.

2. The interpretive problem: what does it mean to extract “Kuiji’s” philosophy of mind?

There is an important methodological caveat. Kuiji’s philosophy of mind is not presented in the form of a modern treatise titled “On Mind.” It is reconstructed from commentarial work on Indian Yogācāra, especially the Cheng weishi lun, and from related discussions of epistemology, language, meditation, karmic seeds, and liberation. Much of what we call “Kuiji’s view” is therefore an East Asian systematization of Indian materials associated with Asaṅga, Vasubandhu, Dignāga, Sthiramati, Dharmapāla, Xuanzang, and others. Kuiji’s originality lies less in inventing all doctrines ex nihilo than in selecting, ranking, defending, and integrating them into a coherent Faxiang system.

A rigorous reconstruction should therefore distinguish four layers.

First, there is inherited Indian Yogācāra: doctrines of consciousness-only, three natures, eight consciousnesses, the store consciousness, seeds, self-awareness, and the transformation of the basis. Second, there is Xuanzang’s transmission, especially his preference for the Dharmapāla interpretation and his rejection of some earlier Chinese Yogācāra readings. Third, there is the Cheng weishi lun as a synthetic Chinese text, shaped by translation, selection, and editorial arrangement. Fourth, there is Kuiji’s own commentarial elaboration, where these doctrines are defended, clarified, and applied to problems such as selfhood, perception, language, religious instruction, and intersubjectivity.

This is why Kuiji should be read neither as merely a “Chinese idealist” nor as simply an “Indian Buddhist scholastic in China.” He occupies a mediating position: a translator-exegete’s philosopher, whose thought emerges from a living controversy over how consciousness, world, self, language, and liberation are to be understood.

3. The central philosophical problem: how can there be experience without a self?

The deepest problem Kuiji inherits is not the modern mind-body problem in its Cartesian form. It is the Buddhist problem of experience under the constraints of no-self, momentariness, karma, and liberation.

Buddhist theories of mind generally reject the idea that mental phenomena belong to a substantial, independent, enduring self. Human beings are analyzed as dependently arisen physical and psychological processes rather than as selves possessing mental states in the way a substance possesses properties. This creates an acute explanatory challenge: if there is no permanent subject, how can there be memory, responsibility, karmic continuity, perception of a stable world, linguistic understanding, affective habit, moral transformation, and the persistence of ignorance across time?

Kuiji’s answer, following Yogācāra, is that continuity does not require a self-substance. It requires a causal stream. Mind is not one thing; it is a structured process. The apparent person is a conventional unity produced by interdependent streams of consciousness, latent seeds, bodily supports, sense faculties, affective habits, and conceptual constructions. The “self” is not a metaphysical owner of consciousness; it is a misrecognized pattern within consciousness.

This immediately differentiates Kuiji from the dominant Western starting point after Descartes. Descartes frames the problem around a thinking substance distinct from extended body, and post-Cartesian philosophy inherits the question of how mental and physical substances or properties relate. Kuiji’s framework does not begin with two substances. It begins with dependently arisen cognitive processes and asks how the illusion of subject-object duality and selfhood is generated and how it can be undone. Descartes shifts the Western problem toward consciousness and the relation between mind and body as distinct substances; Kuiji, by contrast, analyzes the construction of self and object within a no-self, no-substance metaphysics.

4. The architecture of mind: eight consciousnesses

The backbone of Kuiji’s philosophy of mind is the Yogācāra model of eight consciousnesses. These are not eight souls, eight faculties in a modern neurological sense, or eight homunculi. They are functionally differentiated modes of cognitive process.

The first five consciousnesses are the sensory consciousnesses: visual, auditory, olfactory, gustatory, and tactile. They are tied to sense faculties and sensory domains. In Yogācāra epistemology, sense consciousness is often treated as comparatively immediate and nonconceptual. It does not by itself produce the fully articulated object “this is a blue vase,” “this is my enemy,” or “this is a desirable possession.” Such determinate cognition requires further mental processing.

The sixth consciousness is mental consciousness. It coordinates, conceptualizes, remembers, imagines, judges, infers, names, and narrativizes. It is the principal site of ordinary discursive thought. If the first five consciousnesses supply sensory episodes, the sixth consciousness organizes them into a cognitively meaningful world. It is also the locus of many errors, because it overlays conceptual construction onto what appears.

The seventh consciousness is manas, especially kliṣṭamanas, the defiled or afflicted mental function. This is one of the most important components of Kuiji’s philosophy of subjectivity. Manas takes the deeper store consciousness as an object and appropriates it as “I” or “mine.” It is not a self; it is the process through which selfhood is misconstructed. It produces the egocentric orientation around which desire, aversion, pride, fear, and possessiveness organize themselves.

The eighth consciousness is ālayavijñāna, the store or basis consciousness. It is a subliminal causal continuum that stores karmic “seeds,” supports bodily and worldly appearance, and explains continuity without positing a permanent self. It is not an eternal soul. It is a stream, a repository, a causal support, a basis for manifestation, and ultimately something to be transformed.

This architecture lets Kuiji explain several phenomena at once: why perception appears world-directed; why conceptual thought can distort perception; why the sense of self feels deep and pre-reflective; why karmic and affective patterns persist; why memory and habit are possible; why meditative transformation can alter experience at its root; and why liberation requires more than adopting correct propositions.

5. Ālayavijñāna: continuity without self

The store consciousness is perhaps the most philosophically powerful and controversial part of Kuiji’s system. It functions as an answer to a problem that Buddhist philosophy repeatedly faces: how can karmic continuity exist if everything is momentary and there is no self? The store consciousness holds seeds or latent dispositions that can mature into future experiences, actions, tendencies, perceptions, and environments. It is the continuity of a stream, not the identity of a substance.

The seeds are not merely memories in the ordinary sense. They are latent causal potentials. Actions, perceptions, emotions, intentions, and concepts “perfume” the store consciousness; later, these seeds mature into further patterns of cognition and action. This is a sophisticated theory of mental latency. It explains why one’s mind is not exhausted by what is presently conscious. It also explains why transformation is difficult: ignorance is not just an intellectual mistake but a deeply sedimented causal pattern.

The store consciousness also explains embodiment and world-continuity. Yogācāra sources describe it as operative in states where manifest consciousness is suspended or altered, such as deep sleep, unconsciousness, certain meditative absorptions, and rebirth. It is also used to explain why a body and world continue to appear as the stable support of experience. Yet Yogācāra insists that this does not reintroduce an ātman. The store consciousness is impermanent, causally conditioned, morally indeterminate, and transformed or ceases in liberation.

In Western terms, the store consciousness invites comparison with the unconscious, but the comparison must be qualified. Like the unconscious, it accounts for latent mental causality beneath reflective awareness. Unlike the Freudian unconscious, it is not primarily a repository of repressed personal wishes. It is a karmic, cognitive, affective, and existential support for the entire stream of experience. It is closer to a theory of dispositional consciousness than to a clinical theory of repression.

6. Manas: the construction of the subject

Kuiji’s theory of manas is one of his most important contributions to philosophy of mind because it gives a precise account of subject-formation without a subject-substance. The ordinary sense of “I” is not primal. It is constructed by an afflicted cognitive function that takes the store consciousness as a basis and misappropriates it.

This is subtle. The self is not simply a false belief consciously entertained by the sixth consciousness. It is a pre-reflective, affectively charged orientation. Long before a person says “I am this body” or “this is mine,” the mind is organized around appropriation. Manas gives experience a center. It imposes the structure of mineness. It treats a dependently arisen flow as if it were an owner, witness, or controller.

This gives Kuiji an account of egocentric consciousness deeper than propositional error. The self is not refuted merely by argument because the self is not merely an argument. It is a mode of cognitive-affective organization. It is enacted in attention, desire, defensive reaction, comparison, pride, shame, fear, and attachment. That is why meditation and ethical discipline are indispensable: the target is not only belief but the structure of mental causality.

Compared with Hume, Kuiji shares the rejection of a substantial self and the emphasis on a stream or bundle-like sequence of mental events. But Kuiji goes further by explaining why the bundle falsely appears self-owned. Hume famously finds no self beyond perceptions; Kuiji explains the cognitive mechanism that makes a self seem to be present despite the absence of such a self. The seventh consciousness, in this respect, is a theory of subjectification.

7. Consciousness-only: not “everything is in my head”

The term “consciousness-only” is easily misunderstood. The crude interpretation is solipsistic: only my mind exists, and the world is a private hallucination. Kuiji’s position is not that. A better formulation is: what is ordinarily taken as an independently existing subject-object world is in fact world-as-cognized, constituted through dependently arisen cognitive processes and karmic seeds.

Yogācāra is often described through names such as vijñānavāda, cittamātra, and vijñaptimātra. These terms emphasize the priority of consciousness, mind, or representation in the constitution of experienced reality. Modern scholars sometimes compare this with idealism, but Yogācāra is not straightforwardly equivalent to Western metaphysical idealism. It does not posit a permanent mental substance, a divine perceiver, or an eternal ego. Its target is the reification of subject and object.

Kuiji’s consciousness-only should therefore be read as a theory of cognitive objectivity. Objects are not denied as appearances, causal nodes, or conventional referents. What is denied is that ordinary cognition gives us access to objects as self-existent entities standing outside cognitive construction. The world as grasped by ignorance is a falsely objectified world. The subject who grasps it is likewise falsely objectified.

This position has affinities with representational theories of mind, because experience is mediated by cognitive appearances or representations. But Kuiji’s view is not merely that mental states represent external objects. The deeper claim is that the very polarity between representing subject and represented object belongs to a dependent and corrigible structure of consciousness. Contemporary representationalism often asks how mental states represent the world; Kuiji asks how the subject-world polarity itself arises, becomes reified, causes suffering, and can be transformed.

8. The three natures: Kuiji’s ontology of experience

The doctrine of the three natures is indispensable for extracting Kuiji’s philosophy of mind. It gives the metaphysical and phenomenological grammar of his system.

The first nature is the imagined or constructed nature, parikalpita. This is the falsely imposed duality of subject and object, self and world, grasper and grasped. It is not “imaginary” in the trivial sense of being nothing at all. It is imaginary in the sense that it is misconstrued. It is the world as structured by reification.

The second nature is the dependent nature, paratantra. This is the causal flow of consciousness, seeds, conditions, perceptions, actions, and latent tendencies. It is the dependently arisen process that makes experience possible. If the imagined nature is the false overlay, the dependent nature is the conditioned basis upon which the overlay occurs.

The third nature is the perfected nature, pariniṣpanna. This is not another object behind appearances. It is the suchness of the dependent flow when the imagined duality is absent. In other words, perfected nature is dependent nature correctly realized as empty of the falsely imagined subject-object split.

This triad is one of the most elegant parts of Yogācāra philosophy of mind. It prevents two opposite errors. Against naïve realism, it says ordinary subject-object experience is constructed and mistaken. Against nihilism, it says there is still dependent causal process. Against metaphysical absolutism, it says liberation is not finding a separate true object; it is realizing the absence of the imagined in the dependent.

In Western comparison, the three natures partly resemble a distinction between appearance, conditions of appearance, and corrected insight into appearance. But they are not Kantian categories, not Husserlian reductions, and not sense-data theory. They are embedded in a Buddhist analysis of ignorance and liberation.

9. Reflexive awareness and the four aspects of cognition

Kuiji’s Yogācāra inherits and elaborates a sophisticated theory of the internal structure of cognition. In the Dharmapāla line associated with the Cheng weishi lun, cognition is analyzed through four aspects: the appearance of the object, the appearance of the subject or seeing function, self-cognition, and the cognition of self-cognition. This four-aspect model is central to debates over whether consciousness is aware of itself and how cognition can be known without positing a separate observer.

The point is not that there is an ego watching mental states from behind them. Rather, cognition has reflexive structure. A cognitive episode presents something and, in some sense, manifests itself. This is one reason Yogācāra can reject an enduring self without reducing consciousness to blind occurrence. Consciousness is not owned by a self, but it is not opaque to itself either.

This theory is relevant to contemporary debates about self-awareness, higher-order theories of consciousness, and pre-reflective awareness. But Kuiji’s model differs from many Western approaches because it is embedded in a karmic and soteriological theory. Self-awareness is not simply a neutral datum of phenomenology. It can be misused by manas in self-appropriation, or purified in wisdom.

A useful comparison is with higher-order representational theories, which often explain consciousness by saying that a mental state becomes conscious when represented by another mental state. Kuiji’s inherited Yogācāra model is more complex. It allows a cognition to have internal reflexivity without requiring a separate Cartesian subject. At the same time, the fourth aspect suggests an attempt to account for the reliability or certification of self-cognition: not merely cognition, but cognition’s awareness of its own awareness.

10. Perception: directness, construction, and the object

Kuiji’s philosophy of perception must be understood through the distinction between nonconceptual sensory contact and conceptual elaboration. Sense consciousness may be relatively immediate, but ordinary objects are not simply given. The object as named, classified, desired, feared, possessed, or judged is already a product of mental consciousness and latent karmic tendencies.

This resembles some Western distinctions between sensation and judgment, intuition and concept, or perception and interpretation. But Kuiji’s distinction has a Buddhist purpose: it shows how delusion is built into ordinary object cognition. The world is not merely perceived; it is appropriated, categorized, and affectively charged.

The key point is that cognition presents an object-aspect. What appears as an external object is, for consciousness-only, a manifestation within cognition. This does not mean that nothing constrains experience. Seeds, other minds, karmic conditions, bodily conditions, and prior cognitive formations all constrain what appears. But the object that is naively taken to be simply “out there” is, in the relevant sense, object-as-cognized.

This is where Kuiji diverges sharply from both naïve realism and simple subjectivism. Against naïve realism, he denies that ordinary perception directly discloses self-existing external objects. Against subjectivism, he preserves causal structure, intersubjective coordination, and conventional validity.

11. Language and concept formation: Kuiji and Buddhist semantics

Kuiji’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of language. Through Xuanzang’s transmission, Kuiji was deeply involved with Buddhist logic and epistemology associated with Dignāga. One crucial issue is how words relate to objects if particulars are momentary and direct perception is nonconceptual.

The Dignāga theory of apoha, or exclusion, holds that a linguistic item does not refer to a real universal existing in things. Rather, conceptual meaning operates by excluding what is other. “Blue” does not grasp a metaphysical universal blueness; it functions through exclusion of non-blue and through conceptual construction. Kuiji discusses such issues in his commentarial work, including the distinction between particular characteristics, which are the objects of direct perception, and general characteristics, which are the objects of words and conceptual cognition.

This is crucial for philosophy of mind because it means conceptual cognition is not a transparent mirror of reality. Language organizes experience by generalization, exclusion, and construction. It is useful, but it is also a source of reification. Once the mind names and classifies, it tends to treat constructed categories as independently real.

There is also a technical problem of sentence comprehension. If consciousness is momentary, and if sounds arise sequentially, how does the mind understand a whole sentence? Scholarly work on Kuiji’s Yogācāra materials highlights the difficulty of explaining sentence meaning under conditions of momentariness and multiple consciousnesses. The issue concerns how sensory consciousness, mental consciousness, memory, and syntactic structure cooperate so that a sequence of sounds can be apprehended as meaningful speech.

This matters for religious practice. Buddhist teaching depends on language, but language is also conceptually constructed. Kuiji must therefore explain how verbal instruction can function without making words ultimately real. The answer is that language is conventionally effective even if ultimately empty of intrinsic reference. It can activate seeds, reorganize attention, and guide the mind toward nonconceptual realization.

12. Other minds and intersubjectivity: Kuiji is not a solipsist

A common misunderstanding of consciousness-only is that it collapses into solipsism. Kuiji explicitly resists that conclusion. In his account, consciousness-only does not negate other sentient beings. Rather, it denies that the objects of experience are mind-independent in the naïve realist sense. Other mental continua exist conventionally and causally; they can influence one another.

Recent scholarship on Kuiji’s use of adhipati — translated in this context as an activating, amplifying, or dominant causal influence — emphasizes that Kuiji uses it to explain intersubjectivity within a consciousness-only framework. Different streams of consciousness can serve as causal conditions for one another. Teaching and being taught are paradigmatic examples: the Buddha’s preaching, a teacher’s words, or another person’s conduct can causally activate transformations in a hearer’s stream of consciousness.

This is a major philosophical achievement. Kuiji must explain how one mind can affect another if the perceived teacher, words, and world are consciousness-only. His answer is not to posit a single objective material world as the metaphysical ground of communication. Nor is it to reduce all others to projections of one private mind. Instead, he uses a theory of multiple mental continua, remote objects, and causal influence. Other minds are not directly grasped as intimate objects, but they can function as remote conditions for one’s cognition.

This places Kuiji in an interesting relation to Western discussions of the problem of other minds. He does not solve the problem by analogy from one’s own case in the way often discussed in modern epistemology. Nor does he appeal to a shared physical world as the ultimate guarantor of intersubjectivity. He offers a causal-pragmatic model: the reality of other minds is shown by the efficacy of interaction, teaching, response, karmic influence, and shared patterns of experience.

13. The body and the world

Kuiji’s system is not body-denying, but it does not treat the body as an independently existing physical substance in the modern materialist sense. The body is part of the dependently arisen field of cognition. It is supported by seeds, store consciousness, sense faculties, and karmic maturation. It appears as the bodily basis of experience and action.

In Western philosophy, body-mind relations are often framed through substance dualism, materialism, property dualism, or functionalism. Kuiji’s model is different. The body is neither a Cartesian extended substance wholly separate from mind nor a purely physical basis that produces consciousness. It is a karmically conditioned appearance and support within a stream of consciousness-only.

This does not make the body unreal in the sense of useless or nonexistent. The body is conventionally real, causally effective, and ethically important. Pain, illness, discipline, posture, perception, and action all matter. But the body is not ultimately graspable as a self-existing thing. It is part of the dependent nature.

14. Error, affect, and suffering

Kuiji’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from Buddhist soteriology. Mind is not studied simply to describe cognition. It is studied because ordinary cognition is bound up with suffering.

The basic error is not merely factual ignorance. It is the affective-cognitive construction of a self confronting a world of desirable, threatening, and possessible objects. The imagined duality of subject and object feeds craving, aversion, pride, envy, and fear. Manas appropriates; mental consciousness conceptualizes; seeds reinforce patterns; perception appears to confirm the world that ignorance projects.

This produces a feedback loop. Ignorant action perfumes the store consciousness with seeds. Those seeds mature into further distorted appearances and dispositions. The distorted appearances then occasion further ignorant action. Kuiji’s mind theory is therefore recursive and dynamic: cognition conditions action, action conditions latent seeds, seeds condition future cognition.

This is one of the places where Kuiji anticipates themes that modern philosophy of mind and cognitive science often discuss separately: perception is affect-laden, cognition is habit-structured, the self is constructed, and transformation requires intervention below the level of explicit belief. But Kuiji’s framework remains distinct because the ultimate aim is liberation from suffering, not merely accurate representation or adaptive functioning.

15. Transformation of the basis: philosophy of mind as therapy

For Kuiji, the culmination of philosophy of mind is āśraya-parāvṛtti, the transformation of the basis. The “basis” includes the deep structure of consciousness, especially the store consciousness and its seeds. Liberation is not simply the acquisition of new beliefs. It is a restructuring of the causal basis from which experience arises.

This transformation converts defiled consciousness into wisdom. In Yogācāra systems, the eight consciousnesses are often correlated with purified wisdoms: the store consciousness is transformed into great mirror wisdom; manas into equality wisdom; mental consciousness into wondrous observing wisdom; and the five sensory consciousnesses into action-accomplishing wisdom. The exact doctrinal formulations vary, but the underlying philosophical point is clear: the mind’s structure can be transformed from delusive representation to nondual wisdom.

This makes Kuiji’s system a philosophy of cognitive plasticity. Seeds can be perfumed by practice. Attention can be disciplined. Conceptual reification can be weakened. Ethical conduct can alter latent tendencies. Meditation can expose the constructed nature of subject-object duality. Teaching can activate transformation through intersubjective influence.

A Western comparison with psychotherapy is tempting but incomplete. Kuiji’s transformation is not merely therapeutic normalization. It is a radical reconfiguration of the basis of experience. The end is not a well-adjusted ego; it is the cessation of egoic misappropriation.

16. The controversial doctrine of five gotras

Kuiji is also associated with one of the most controversial Faxiang doctrines: the five gotras, or five spiritual lineages/capacities. In contrast to traditions that emphasize universal Buddha-nature and the eventual Buddhahood of all beings, Faxiang sources linked to Kuiji defend a more differentiated account of spiritual capacity. Some beings are oriented toward śrāvaka liberation, some toward pratyekabuddha liberation, some toward bodhisattva Buddhahood, some have indeterminate capacity, and some are described as lacking the seeds required for enlightenment.

This doctrine must be read carefully. It is not a simple biological determinism or social caste theory. It is a theory of karmic seeds and spiritual capacities. Faxiang sources also complicate the idea of the icchantika, the being supposedly deprived of the requisite seeds, by treating it in relation to desire for enlightenment rather than as a crude permanent essence. Still, the doctrine sharply contrasts with universalist Buddha-nature traditions and became one reason Faxiang was criticized.

For philosophy of mind, the five-gotra doctrine means Kuiji does not assume all minds have the same latent structure or developmental trajectory. Minds differ in seeds, capacities, and possible paths. This gives his theory a strong dispositional and pedagogical dimension: teaching must be adapted to the deep causal structure of the learner’s mind.

The ethical risk is obvious. A doctrine of unequal spiritual capacity can harden into exclusion. The philosophical value is also clear: Kuiji takes seriously the fact that minds are not abstractly identical. They are historically conditioned, karmically differentiated, and transformed through different paths.

17. Kuiji and Western philosophy of mind: convergences and contrasts

Kuiji’s philosophy of mind can be compared with several Western developments, but it should not be assimilated to any of them.

With Descartes, Kuiji shares the centrality of consciousness. But Descartes frames mind as a thinking substance distinct from body, whereas Kuiji rejects a substantial self and analyzes consciousness as a dependently arisen stream. The Cartesian problem is how mind and body interact; Kuiji’s problem is how self and object are constructed within mind and how that construction can be transformed.

With Berkeley, Kuiji shares the denial that ordinary material objects exist independently in the way naïve realism supposes. But Berkeley’s idealism is built around minds, ideas, and God. Kuiji’s consciousness-only lacks an eternal soul, a divine guarantor, and a stable perceiving ego. It also aims at dismantling subject-object duality, not securing the existence of the world through divine perception. Western idealism is itself diverse, spanning epistemological and metaphysical forms; Kuiji’s view is best treated as a Buddhist theory of mind-dependent appearance and cognitive construction, not as a direct analogue of Berkeleyan immaterialism.

With Hume, Kuiji shares an anti-substantialist account of selfhood. Hume’s bundle theory and Buddhist no-self have often been compared. But Kuiji’s system is more architecturally elaborate: it explains the persistence of habit through seeds, the pre-reflective construction of self through manas, and the deep causal continuity of experience through ālayavijñāna. Hume dismantles the self; Kuiji explains how the self-illusion is generated and how it can be undone.

With Kant, Kuiji shares the idea that experienced objects are not simply mind-independent things as they are in themselves. But Kant’s framework turns on universal a priori conditions of possible experience, whereas Kuiji’s turns on karmic seeds, dependent arising, and transformable cognitive structures. Kant secures objectivity by analyzing the necessary conditions of experience; Kuiji deconstructs reified objectivity in order to end suffering.

With Husserlian phenomenology, Kuiji shares concern with intentionality, appearance, subjectivity, and intersubjectivity. Husserl’s work analyzes intentionality, temporality, embodiment, and the lifeworld, and it profoundly shaped modern philosophy of mind and cognitive science. Yet Kuiji’s tradition does not simply perform phenomenological description. As Robert Sharf has argued about the Cheng weishi lun, its categories often focus on subliminal processes and metaphysical entities rather than on lived experience in the Husserlian sense. Kuiji is therefore phenomenologically relevant, but not simply a phenomenologist.

With functionalism, Kuiji shares the idea that mental categories can be understood by role and operation rather than by substance. The eight consciousnesses are functionally differentiated: sensory presentation, conceptual coordination, self-appropriation, latent storage, and so on. But modern functionalism defines mental states by causal roles, often in a physicalist or computational context; Kuiji’s roles are karmic, ethical, contemplative, and soteriological. Functionalism as a fully articulated Western theory belongs to the late twentieth century, whereas Kuiji’s theory is a seventh-century Buddhist account of mental function within a liberation-oriented metaphysics.

With contemporary representational theories of consciousness, Kuiji shares the idea that consciousness involves appearances or contents. But many representational theories try to explain consciousness in terms of intentional content, higher-order representation, or tracking relations. Kuiji instead treats representation itself as part of the problem: representation becomes delusive when it is reified into a subject facing an independently existing object.

18. Kuiji’s philosophy of mind as a systematic set of theses

A comprehensive reconstruction of Kuiji’s philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses. 1. Mind is process, not substance. Consciousness is a stream of momentary, dependently arisen events, not the activity of an eternal self. 2. The person is conventionally real but ultimately non-substantial. Personal continuity is explained by causal continuity, not by identity of a soul. 3. Consciousness is stratified. The mind includes sensory consciousness, conceptual mental consciousness, self-appropriating manas, and store consciousness. 4. Ordinary selfhood is constructed. The sense of “I” arises when manas appropriates the store consciousness as self. 5. Experience is representational but not realist in the naïve sense. Objects appear as cognitive manifestations; ordinary cognition falsely treats them as independently existing. 6. Latent dispositions structure experience. Seeds stored in ālayavijñāna condition perception, affect, action, memory, and future experience. 7. Cognition is reflexive. Consciousness is not merely directed outward; it includes structures of self-awareness, though these do not imply an ego. 8. Concepts do not grasp ultimate reality. Language operates through generalization, exclusion, and convention; it is useful but prone to reification. 9. Other minds are real as causally effective continua. Consciousness-only is not solipsism; intersubjective influence is explained through causal conditioning such as adhipati. 10. The world is shared through karmic and causal coordination, not through an ultimately independent material substrate. 11. Ignorance is affective-cognitive. Error is not merely false belief; it is a deep structure of self-appropriation, objectification, craving, and aversion. 12. Philosophy of mind is inseparable from ethics. Actions perfume the store consciousness; moral conduct reshapes the causal basis of future experience. 13. Liberation is transformation of cognition. The goal is not theoretical correspondence with an external world but transformation of the basis of consciousness. 14. Nonduality is not the denial of experience. It is the realization that the imagined duality of subject and object is absent from dependent arising. 15. Minds differ in latent capacities. Kuiji’s five-gotra doctrine makes spiritual development dependent on karmic seeds and dispositions, though this remains ethically and doctrinally controversial.

19. Historical importance in the global philosophy of mind

Kuiji deserves a more central place in the global history of philosophy of mind for at least five reasons.

First, he offers a highly developed theory of unconscious or subliminal mental causality long before modern European theories of the unconscious. Ālayavijñāna is not Freud, but it is a sophisticated account of latent dispositions and nonconscious conditioning.

Second, he offers a theory of self-construction that avoids both substantialism and eliminative triviality. The self is not real as an enduring entity, but the self-illusion is real as an operative cognitive-affective pattern.

Third, he integrates perception, language, and metaphysics. His account of conceptual construction and exclusion shows how linguistic cognition contributes to the reification of the world.

Fourth, he preserves intersubjectivity without naïve realism. His use of causal influence among mental continua gives him resources against solipsism while maintaining consciousness-only.

Fifth, his philosophy of mind is intrinsically transformative. It is not merely descriptive or explanatory. It is therapeutic in the deepest Buddhist sense: to understand mind is to understand how suffering is generated and how liberation is possible.

20. Critical assessment

Kuiji’s system is powerful, but it faces serious philosophical pressures.

The first is the ālaya problem. Store consciousness solves continuity, but it risks looking like a disguised self. Yogācāra responds by insisting that ālayavijñāna is impermanent, conditioned, morally variable, and transformable. Whether this fully avoids the charge depends on how strictly one defines selfhood.

The second is the other-minds problem. Kuiji avoids solipsism by positing multiple mental continua and causal influence among them. But critics may ask whether this already presupposes the intersubjectivity it is meant to explain. The doctrine of adhipati is elegant, but it does not satisfy every modern demand for epistemic access to other minds.

The third is the status of the external world. Is Kuiji denying external objects ontologically, or only denying that they are experienced apart from cognitive construction? Yogācāra scholarship remains divided between stronger idealist and more phenomenological readings. Kuiji’s Faxiang position is relatively strong in its consciousness-only commitments, but it still should not be flattened into Berkeleyan idealism or private subjectivism.

The fourth is the five-gotra doctrine. As a psychology of differing capacities, it is philosophically interesting. As a doctrine that appears to deny universal Buddhahood, it is doctrinally and ethically troubling, especially in contrast with Buddha-nature traditions.

The fifth is the limited role of the brain or physical embodiment in modern terms. Kuiji has a rich theory of body-as-experienced and body-as-karmic support, but he does not offer anything like neuroscience. His theory is not a competitor to contemporary brain science at the empirical level. Its value lies elsewhere: in phenomenology, metaphysics, moral psychology, and contemplative transformation.

Conclusion

Kuiji’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a layered, anti-substantialist, consciousness-only account of experience in which mind is a dynamic stream structured by sensory presentation, conceptual construction, self-appropriation, latent seeds, intersubjective influence, and transformative practice. It explains how a selfless process can appear as a self confronting a world; how karmic and affective patterns persist without a soul; how language constructs general objects; how other minds can matter without naïve realism; and how liberation requires transforming the deep basis of cognition.

In one sentence: Kuiji’s mind is not a Cartesian substance, not a Berkeleyan private theater, not a Kantian transcendental ego, not a Humean bundle alone, and not a modern computational system; it is a karmically conditioned, self-reflexive, multi-layered, intersubjectively affected process that misrecognizes itself as subject facing object and can be transformed into nondual wisdom.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
